_readme: Each entry is a regex checked against the FULL command string before any permission prompt. If a pattern matches, the tool call is rejected immediately -- no allow-once, no allow-session, no override. Keep this list to things that are catastrophic and essentially never legitimate for a recon agent to run. Anything merely 'risky but sometimes needed' belongs in the permission-gate flow instead, not here.

shell.reason: Recursive force delete -- classic 'rm -rf /' or 'rm -rf ~' pattern. Irreversible mass data loss.
Fork bomb -- ':(){ :|:& };:' spawns processes until the system dies.
Formats a filesystem/partition -- destroys all data on the target device.
Raw disk write with dd -- can overwrite an entire physical disk.
Recursively opens permissions on the root filesystem -- system-wide privilege/security collapse.
Recursive ownership change on root filesystem -- can break the OS entirely.
Redirecting output directly onto a raw block device -- corrupts the device.
Powers off or restarts the host machine the agent is running on.
Classic 'curl | bash' remote code execution pattern -- runs arbitrary untrusted code from the network with no review.
Same risk as curl-pipe-to-shell, via wget.
Changes a user password on the host -- could lock out operators or escalate access.
Flushes or disables the host firewall -- removes network protections mid-engagement.
Destroys audit trails -- an agent should never be able to erase its own logs or shell history.

http.reason: Cloud metadata service (AWS/GCP/Azure IMDS). Fetching this is SSRF against the agent's own host, not the target -- can leak cloud credentials.
Blocks the agent from silently pivoting HTTP calls back onto the machine it's running on.
file:// scheme in an HTTP tool call can be used to read local files via a library that supports it -- treat as an attempted local file read in disguise.

file.reason: Core OS auth/privilege files. Reading is a credential-harvesting risk, writing can grant root.
SSH private keys and authorized_keys -- writing here can plant persistent access; reading exfiltrates keys.
Deep path traversal sequence -- almost always an attempt to escape the intended working directory.
